source_label: Axis Spitfire Review | Excellent for Wing Foil and Prone Foil
text: Cole's favorite prone foil out of all brands they carry at the shop. Blown away by turning performance — edge-to-edge rolls are immediate and the foil pumps well enough to connect 3-4 waves without getting gassed. The constant chord width across the span means turbulence only causes side-to-side pitch, not nose-up/down pitching, making it extremely forgiving in rough water.
key_insight: Constant chord width design prevents nose pitch changes in turbulent water — only causes manageable side-to-side movement, making it uniquely forgiving among foils.

source_label: Axis Spitfire Review | Excellent for Wing Foil and Prone Foil
text: Excellent low-end and stall speed for winging — can stall almost to nothing and pick up the wing again. Top end speed limit is reachable when cooking upwind on the wing, but the speed range is impressive for wave riding. Pairs well with the 365 Skinny stabilizer for higher-speed winging performance. Best suited for wingers who want to carve waves with tight top-to-bottom turns.
key_insight: Exceptional stall speed recovery — you can nearly stall out completely and still pick up and restart without falling off foil, unlike most wings that dump you in the water.

source_label: Axis Spitfire Vs ART | The Foil Wing Comparison You'll Want
text: Clear winner for carving and turning — immediate edge-to-edge response compared to ART's slow, patient turns. Surprisingly matched ART's top-end speed in waist-high waves during prone, though winging speed was close. Massive advantage in stall speed for winging and in wingouts (half the wing out of water and still ripping through turns). Low-end popup is much easier than ART for winging but slightly slower to activate than ART when prone foiling, with some instability at very low speeds.
key_insight: Spitfire matched or exceeded ART's top-end speed in wave conditions despite lower aspect ratio — riders go faster than they realize because it's so smooth and forgiving.

source_label: Axis Spitfire Vs ART | The Foil Wing Comparison You'll Want
text: Clear winner for pumping — high aspect design glides with minimal effort and pumps at higher speeds than Spitfire. Better for downwind swell riding and gliding at speed. More stable at very low activation speeds when prone but drops out more suddenly once you start to slow down. Turning requires much more patience and rider input — you really have to throw your weight around. Wingouts cause the whole leading edge to suck air and crash.
key_insight: ART is the clear choice for downwind-style riding and pumping efficiency, but loses badly to Spitfire in turning responsiveness and stall speed forgiveness.

text: The Spitfire 900 handles wingtip breaches effortlessly — what used to require emergency recovery procedures now barely registers. Pitch control is extraordinary across all speeds and maneuvers; you almost don't have to think about it. Pumping requires a completely different technique: avoid high angle of attack, use shallow down-up motions, spring up and bring knees to chest for glide, and stay in the top third of the mast. Faster than HPS but not as fast as ART. Not for beginners — too performance-oriented despite being user-friendly.
key_insight: Wingtip breach handling is a game-changer — the foil barely reacts to breaches that would crash other foils, enabling fearless aggressive surfing.

text: Smaller sizes like the 780 become incredibly reactive and fast for direction changes on the roll axis. As sizes decrease, the Spitfires get more snappy and responsive for cutbacks and slashy turns. Mentioned as potentially fun for winging on fast-moving rolling swell, though Dominic hadn't tested that combination yet.
key_insight: Smaller Spitfire sizes amplify the series' turning responsiveness — the 780 is extremely reactive for quick direction changes and slashy turns.

text: For winging downwind, Spitfires work best in peaky, slow-moving, short-period wind swell or bay runs. They cannot keep up with fast-moving low ocean groundswell — the ART is far better for those conditions. Choose Spitfire over ART for winging only when using the wing to access small-medium waves for aggressive carving, not for speed/glide downwinding. More lift than equivalent-sized ARTs, making them easier to get on foil.
key_insight: Spitfires are condition-specific for winging: choose them for peaky slow swell where you want aggressive carving, but stick with ART for fast ocean groundswell.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Compared to ART 999 (similar surface area), the 840 needs more speed to get up but once flying everything becomes easier — transitions are more forgiving and it naturally keeps you in the pocket of the wave rather than drifting to the shoulder. Provides noticeably more lift than ART, requiring front-foot discipline on steep waves. Perfect setup: Crazy Short fuselage + 325 Progressive tail. Felt draggy above 25 knots, prompting a step down to 780. Sold the setup to a friend who came from Takuma foils and instantly loved it.
key_insight: The 840's extra lift forces you to ride in the wave's pocket rather than the shoulder — it fundamentally changes wave-riding style from gliding to true surfing, but feels draggy above 25 knots.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Became the reviewer's go-to all-conditions wingfoil front wing. In 25+ knot conditions where the 840 felt draggy, the 780 restored full control. Surprisingly, when wind dropped it didn't feel meaningfully different from the 840 in power needed to get going. Even more maneuverable than the 840 for wave riding. Surface area of ~900cm² makes it not actually small for riders under 80kg. Best setup: Advanced Crazy Short fuselage + Skinny 360 45 rear wing.
key_insight: The 780 became the everyday wing — handles 25+ knot conditions the 840 can't, yet barely sacrifices low-end performance, making it the sweet spot for advanced wingfoilers.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Tested in 30-knot conditions on arrival day. Getting up on foil was surprisingly easy with barely noticeable difference from the 780 (benefit of stepping down incrementally). Surface area of 815cm² — a good size for lighter riders in strong wind. Excellent for jumping due to the thick profile providing lift. Transitions require noticeably faster reflexes than larger sizes. Best setup: Advanced Crazy Short fuselage + Skinny 360 45 rear wing.
key_insight: At 815cm² the 720 is a viable strong-wind wing that's also excellent for jumping thanks to the Spitfire's thick profile — lighter riders might want even smaller.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Gets up on foil as quickly as the ART 999 despite being a different design. Once flying, it has so much lift that it's almost impossible to drop off foil — even after jumps it pops right back up. However, the usable wind range is narrower than the ART; as wind picks up the 900 can't convert power into speed effectively, requiring a step down. Surface area of 1080cm². Light-wind wave riding is still magic but for all-conditions light-wind use, the ART 999 remains preferred.
key_insight: The 900's massive lift makes it nearly impossible to fall off foil (even after jumps), but its usable wind range is narrower than the ART 999 — you'll need to size down sooner as wind increases.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Remains the go-to light-wind wing and all-conditions choice. Gets on foil quicker than similar-sized Spitfires and has a wider usable wind/speed range. More slippery and less forgiving than Spitfire in transitions — easier to stall with quick angle changes. ART's glide tends to pull riders toward riding the shoulder rather than the pocket of the wave. Not as forgiving as Spitfire for learning wave riding but better for those who want range and speed.
key_insight: ART 999's wider usable range makes it the better all-conditions wing, but its slippery nature subtly trains bad wave-riding habits — riders drift to the shoulder instead of staying in the pocket.

source_label: Axis Foils Spitfire 900 / 840 / 780 / 720 review for wingfoiling
text: Recommended as the ideal choice for riders new to wave riding and beginning wingfoilers. The larger HPS sizes are among the easiest foils to get started with — great for first turns and building confidence. Positioned as the progression path before moving to Spitfire for advanced wave riding.
key_insight: HPS is the recommended entry point for new wingfoilers — easiest AXIS foil to learn on with the widest beginner-friendly range, before graduating to Spitfire.
